nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and rubble areas of outer lagoons, seaward reefs, and estuaries, typically at depths of 2–43 m, generally found sharing a burrow with an alpheid (pistol) shrimp

decorPreferences: live rock with rubble at base

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivorous diet of mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped fresh or frozen marine seafood. Has been reported to do best with at least two small feedings per day. Sifts sand and picks small invertebrates from the burrow entrance in the wild.

avoidWith: other Amblyeleotris individuals unless introduced as a confirmed pair
large crustaceans that may compete for substrate

summary: The Steinitz Goby (Amblyeleotris steinitzi) is a small, peaceful Indo-Pacific shrimp-goby that generally forms a burrow-sharing partnership with a pistol shrimp. Considered beginner-friendly in a quiet reef tank with a deep sand bed and a tightly-sealed lid.
careNotes:
Amblyeleotris steinitzi is a member of the shrimp-goby group: in the wild it almost always shares a burrow with an alpheid pistol shrimp (typically Alpheus djeddensis or related species). The shrimp excavates and maintains the burrow while the near-blind shrimp keeps an antenna on the goby, which acts as a lookout and bolts into the burrow at any threat — the shrimp follows. Pairing one of these gobies with a pistol shrimp in captivity is generally optional but tends to bring out the species' most interesting natural behavior.

A deep sand bed (commonly advised at 5–8 cm or more) is generally considered essential, ideally with mixed rubble at the base of rockwork so the pair has structure to dig under. Without burrow material the goby is reported to be stressed and may stay reclusive.

Like many small reef gobies, this species has been reported to jump from open-top aquariums; a tightly-sealed lid or mesh top is generally advised. It is otherwise considered hardy and fairly disease-resistant.

Generally peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but has been known to be territorial toward conspecifics — best kept singly or as a confirmed pair introduced simultaneously. A larger goby or another shrimp-goby species in the same tank may also draw aggression.

Water parameter notes: Temperature recommendations varied across sources. LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), Reeflings 24–26°C, and FishBase's environmental model 25.2–29°C (mean 27.7°C); the averaged 23–27°C range was used. pH was consistent across LiveAquaria and Reeflings at 8.1–8.4. Salinity was 1.020–1.025 (LiveAquaria) and 1.022–1.025 (Reeflings); averaged to SG 1.021–1.025. Tank size: LiveAquaria cites 10 gallons (~38 L) while Reeflings recommends 75 L (~20 gal); the larger value was chosen because shrimp-gobies need a long sandy footprint with rockwork above for the burrow partnership, and LiveAquaria's number reflects the goby's body size rather than the substrate-and-burrow footprint that the species actually needs.

Adult size: LiveAquaria lists a 5-inch (≈12.7 cm) maximum; Reeflings cites 12 cm; FishBase records 13.0 cm SL. Aquarium specimens are commonly reported in the 8–12 cm range at maturity.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity, though successful reproduction is generally considered uncommon. Forms monogamous pairs that lay eggs inside the shared burrow; the male is reported to guard the clutch. Larval rearing has not been widely documented in the hobby and is generally considered the limiting step.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Direct product page for the Steinitz Goby. Provided maximum size (5 in / 12.7 cm), minimum tank (10 gal / ~38 L), care level (Easy), temperament (Peaceful), reef compatible (Yes), diet (Carnivore), water conditions (pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, 72–78°F), family (Gobiidae), origin (rubble zones of reefs, Indian Ocean), and behavioral notes (sand burrows, pistol shrimp symbiosis, jumping risk).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gobiidae, Gobiiformes), maximum size (13.0 cm SL), wide native distribution (Indo-Pacific: Red Sea to Samoa, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to Great Barrier Reef, throughout Micronesia), habitat (sandy areas of outer lagoon and seaward reefs, estuaries), depth range (2–43 m), environmental temperature model (25.2–29°C, mean 27.7°C), trophic level (3.4, carnivorous), and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2018). Confirmed obligate association with pale brown or marbled brown-and-white alpheid shrimp.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (12 cm), minimum tank (75 L / 20 gal — used over LiveAquaria's 10 gal because of footprint and pistol-shrimp burrow needs), temperature (24–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.022–1.025), care level (moderate to easy), temperament (peaceful), reef safe (yes), native range (Red Sea and Western Indian Ocean), diet (mysis and brine shrimp), pistol shrimp mutualism, monogamous pairing, and rare captive spawning.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs have been reported to spawn occasionally inside the shared burrow with male guarding; larval rearing has not been widely documented and is generally considered the limiting step.
